data.title: Buy The Dip: 9-13% Yields Getting Dirt Cheap
data.description:
I love buying dividend stocks that go on deep discounts, especially when the underlying fundamentals are very strong. The reason for this is that not only do I get a very attractive current income stream that can accelerate my path towards financial independence, but I can also potentially retire early. Dividend income frees me from being dependent on market volatility, and it also gives me significant long-term total return performance potential, due to the fact that you can combine high current yield with some growth potential and then valuation multiple expansion potential as the market eventually realizes that it has been way too bearish on these securities.

With this in mind, in this article, I'm going to discuss two opportunities that yield between 9% and 13% that I think have gotten way too cheap on their recent pullbacks.

A Deep-Discount BDC Hiding in Plain Sight

The first opportunity we're going to discuss is Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). OBDC is a business development company (BIZD) managed by Blue Owl Capital (OWL) and is focused on direct lending to U.S. middle market companies. It currently trades at a whopping 24% discount to its net asset value and offers a very juicy 13.1% dividend yield. The company recently had a large slice of its marks validated due to a $1.4 billion sale at around par value of Blue Owl loans that included a significant portion that were held at OBDC. Additionally, OBDC recently issued a considerable number of investment-grade corporate bonds that were entirely bought out by PIMCO, which is another vote of confidence in the fund's financial strength.

This is also seen with its relatively conservative posture, with 79% of the portfolio invested in senior secured loans, including 73.1% of the portfolio in senior secured loans. It is also well diversified across 234 portfolio companies spread across 30 industries, and its top 10 positions only make up 23% of its fair value. Not only that, but its non-accruals only come at 1.1% at fair value and 2.3% at cost, which are very competitive with many of its BDC peers, like Ares Capital Corp. (ARCC). The lion's share of the portfolio is performing well, with 90.7% of it rated either 1 or 2 and only 1.4% of it rated 4 or 5 on the risk rating scale, meaning that it has a limited risk of a significant ballooning in its non-accruals.

The company also has 12% of the portfolio invested in specialty finance and joint venture opportunities that are generating very high returns on equity of 14% plus, which helped to offset headwinds from recent Federal Reserve rate cuts that have reduced its net investment income. Meanwhile, it does have significant software (IGV) loan exposure; however, its loan-to-value ratio on its software loans is around 30%, which gives it strong downside protection even if these software businesses do suffer due to AI disruption.

The balance sheet is also in sound shape, with it recently being upgraded to Baa2 by Moody's back in January, which gives it one of the highest credit ratings in the entire BDC sector. It has $4.2 billion in total liquidity, with a weighted average term to maturity of 4.3 years, with most of its maturities laddered to 2029 and beyond, and a debt-to-equity of 1.19 times, below its 1.25 times target ceiling, which gives it room to maneuver to potentially add additional leverage to either buy back stock at a steep discount to NAV or invest in attractive opportunities that come its way. In fact, the company has been executing aggressively on share buybacks. It bought back $148 million worth of stock in Q4, which delivered a substantial 5 cents per share NAV accretion for the quarter. It recently expanded its authorization to $300 million, which gives it significant flexibility to continue buying back stock at an aggressive clip.

Navigating Dividend Cut Risk

Perhaps one of the biggest cons to OBDC right now is that it is likely going to cut its dividend. Its base dividend of 37 cents per share slightly exceeded net investment income of 36 cents per share this past quarter, and the company will likely continue to face some headwinds from interest rate changes as well as some potential non-accruals. However, buybacks are a way that it can help offset some of this headwind. Nevertheless, I do expect them to trim their dividend by 10 to 20% to create a more sustainable floor to the dividend and likely will add a supplemental dividend on top of that, as it has done in the past and as peers like Golub Capital BDC (GBDC) have done recently.

There are reasons to like it, however, beyond just the strong underlying fundamentals and the deep discount. For example, insiders have been buying fairly aggressively over the past six months, and management has reiterated that buybacks are an attractive use of capital. The fact that it is actually buying back stock aggressively is a strong signal, in contrast to many other externally managed peers like FS KKR Capital Corp. (FSK), which have not been, despite trading at even larger discounts to NAV. Given that OBDC is externally managed, the management team therefore has little incentive to buy back stock, given that it reduces the fees that they earn from the BDC and takes valuable permanent capital off the table. A company like this that buys back stock so aggressively is sending a very powerful signal that it has high conviction in the inherent value of its portfolio.

OBDC is certainly not risk-free, as there's always the chance that it could suffer a small sell-off in the wake of a dividend cut (though this has likely already been priced in, as evidenced by how other BDCs have responded to similar dividend cuts in the recent past). Additionally, if OBDC sees an uptick in non-accruals, that would certainly at least provide some justification for the market's bearishness on it. However, the discount now has gotten so large, especially relative to the current fundamentals of the company, that this appears to be overblown in my view. Thus, I view it as a strong buy on the recent sell-off.

Energy at a Price the Market Got Wrong

The other opportunity we're going to discuss is Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP), which is a K1-issuing LP that is involved in the coal, oil, and gas royalty business. It generates the majority of its revenue from coal, although it has a rapidly growing oil and gas royalties business that is helping to diversify its income away from coal, giving the company a much longer runway than the market appears to be currently giving it credit for. Additionally, investors can collect a tax-advantaged income yield that is near 10%. While near-term revenues have declined slightly year-over-year due to the roll-off of legacy contracts locked in the immediate aftermath of the Russian invasion of Ukraine in 2022, the business is positioned to inflect towards growth moving forward, as its coal business is 95% contracted for the year and is benefiting from strong demand for base power to support the data center build-out across the country.

Additionally, its oil and gas royalty business is benefiting from the elevated energy pricing in the wake of the Iran war and Hormuz Strait closure. When combined with aggressive acquisitions and strong returns on investment that the company is generating in the oil and gas royalty business, it seems likely that ARLP is going to continue growing its cash flow over time. Moreover, the balance sheet is in phenomenal shape, with a Net Leverage Ratio of just 0.69 times and $431.2 million in liquidity. Finally, if you include the around $45 million worth of Bitcoin on the balance sheet, the Net Leverage Ratio falls considerably further, and the liquidity for the company is considerably higher.

Why Risks Are Overblown and Its Upside Is Overlooked

Of course, ARLP does have risks stemming from its significant coal exposure and the strong political opposition to coal by elements of the Democratic Party, which polls indicate will likely have a strong showing in the upcoming midterm elections. However, strong data center-fueled demand for affordable base power, as well as ARLP's robust growth in its natural gas and oil royalties business, helps to mitigate these threats. This is also aided by its very strong balance sheet. Overall, between its near 10% dividend distribution yield and its increasing diversification and growth prospects, along with its strong balance sheet, I think ARLP is a fairly attractive buy at its recent dip, especially given the changing global dynamics that make U.S. energy more attractive than ever.

The Two-Stock Income Engine That Could Supercharge Your Path to Early Retirement

OBDC and ARLP investors can get pretty broad diversification with exposure to energy and natural resources on the one hand, while getting exposure to a broadly diversified portfolio of senior secured debt investments on the other. On a combined basis, you get a very attractive income yield along with significant capital appreciation potential as well. As a result, I rate both of these opportunities as attractive buys and exactly the type of opportunities we look to capitalize on in our 7 to 8% yielding, total return-outperforming, below-market beta portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Amazon Stock Faces A Post-Earnings Unwind
data.description:
Amazon.com, Inc.’s (AMZN) stock has surged nearly 30% since late March to an all-time high. The setup into earnings suggests that the move is at risk of being unwound quickly.

While the majority of investors will focus on whether results were good or bad, what may matter more is that options positioning heading into these results is overwhelmingly bullish, and the implied volatility is very high. Positioning in the options market may determine the stock's direction.

Solid Results

The company is expected to post strong results on April 29th, with first-quarter revenue rising 13.9% to $177.3 billion and earnings rising 3% to $1.64 per share. CapEx, on the other hand, is expected to surge by 65.5% to $41.4 billion. For the full year, CapEx is expected to rise 44% to $189.8 billion.

Investors will pay close attention to Amazon Web Services, or AWS, which is expected to grow by 25.1% to $36.6 billion in the first quarter and by 25.9% to $38.9 billion in the second quarter.

AWS growth and CapEx spending are clearly the two most important line items for investors when reviewing the results. Unless results materially exceed expectations, the unwind in implied volatility and positioning is likely to dominate the price action.

Implied Volatility Is Very High

Implied volatility for $260 strike price call options expiring on May 1 is currently about 98%, and the premium for that call option is around $9.50 per contract. The buyer of the call needs the stock to trade above $270 per share to start earning a profit following the results, a gain of about 4%.

Once the company reports results and the implied falls, which in Amazon's case have been significant and have historically fallen back into the 30 to 40% range, the call options may only be worth about $3. If the stock is trading below $260 following the results, the options would essentially be worthless.

The decline in IV doesn't just happen in the one-option series; it happens across the chain for both puts and calls because implied volatility affects all option premiums. However, because overall options positions are overly bullish, there will be more decay in call premiums than in put premiums.

Premium Decay

That will have negative impacts on the stock price, the first being that all calls above $260 will decay in value, and the call holders will lose money unless the stock rises above $270. But more importantly, there is a very large amount of call delta built up at $270, so if the stock can't clear $270, those call options, and the options priced higher, are likely to be sold by current holders.

It also means that market makers will find themselves over-hedged relative to the call positioning and will have stock to sell following the results as premium values decline.

The gamma profile suggests a lot of resistance built up in the stock between $260 and $275, with call gamma very heavy. Meanwhile, because the stock has so much call gamma exposure, market-maker hedging flows are likely to go against Amazon's rising, suggesting that market makers are sellers of the stock on rallies and buyers on dips.

Technical View

The technical chart does reveal much of the same information as the option gamma positioning. Currently, the $260 area is serving as both support and resistance. Meanwhile, with a break of support, the next support level comes at the gap created on April 14 at $240, with more support down at another gap from April 8 at $220.

With the stock extended, implied volatility elevated, and call positioning crowded, the setup favors a post-earnings unwind. Without a clear upside surprise, the path of least resistance is lower.

data.title: Seanergy Maritime: The Capesize Pureplay Trading At A Substantial Discount And With Rising Dividends
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Seanergy Maritime Holdings Corp. (SHIP) is a capesize pure play, perfectly positioned to benefit from the rise in rates. The company’s dividend policy should substantially increase the dividends it pays, helping to reduce the valuation gap with its NAV and peers.

Background

Seanergy Maritime is a drybulk company, one of the only two public pure plays in the capesize segment.

Right now its fleet consists of 20 Capesizes with an average age of above 14 years; however, it has sold two of its oldest 2010 Capesizes with delivery in the next few months. To further reduce its fleet age, the company had 3 newbuilds with delivery starting in 2027, and it has recently added two newbuilds with deliveries scheduled for 2027 as well.

Seanergy was founded in 2008 and filed for Chapter 11 in 2012 after the financial crisis collapse. Stamatis Tsantanis took over as CEO in 2012 and restructured the company via an equity raise and still owns about 10% of the company. In 2022, Seanergy spun off its oldest vessel and created United Maritime Corporation (USEA). However, despite some market distrust for this movement and some previous capital raises, management has behaved correctly since then. In fact, they have increased the fleet by 80% since 2020, and since the new dividend policy was established in 2022, they have paid more than $50 million in dividends. The dividend policy was updated in 2024:

And while it states that the company will pay 50% of its operating cash flow, in reality, it is a discretionary dividend because it has a discretionary quarterly reserve.

Besides the CEO’s stake, other Greek investors have significant ownership, like Costamare Inc. (CMRE) CEO, with around a 10% stake, and George Economou’s stake of around 9%. In fact, Economou’s stake prompted a poison pill by Seanergy in December 2023 to protect against a takeover offer well below NAV.

The company charters most of its vessels on index-linked time charters with the possibility to fix them based on the FFA curve. This explains why Seanergy’s realized rates are really close to the BCI.

In the graph above, one can note that Q1 is almost always the softest month of the year; however, this Q1 has been strong. In fact, I expect the company’s Q1 realized rate to be slightly above $24,500, not far from its $26,600 from Q4. In fact, Q2 has started with an important surge, which is a good indication for the rest of the year:

Financial Position and Valuation

Seanergy has a strong financial position with $62 million in cash and $290 million in debt, which is much lower than its vessels’ value, which is $506 million in book value and around $800 million in market value.

Moreover, despite the newbuilds, with the two vessel sales and strong market rates, I don’t expect leverage to increase meaningfully.

The company has full exposure to the capesize rates, implying significant upside if rates remain strong:

For reference, the strongest year for the company was 2024, when it achieved $98.4 million, and this year is poised to break that.

As mentioned above, I already expect a strong Q1 given management guidance of 77% of days fixed at $24,700 per day on average and the rates evolution since then. My Q1 earnings estimate is slightly above $0.5 EPS, higher than analysts’ estimates:

I also expect that the company will maintain its Q4 dividend of $0.2, with further increases in the future if earnings rise as expected.

Regarding valuation, the company is trading at a substantial discount to NAV, around 50%.

While the company has historically traded at a significant discount, I believe that if the dry bulk market is as strong as the futures suggests:

I believe that the company could pay close to $1.5 per share in dividends for 2026. That implies a forward yield of about 10%, higher than usual. Assuming a 7% yield would imply a fair value of around $21, or 0.75x NAV, which I consider correct.

Risks

There are two main risks. The first one is that rates disappoint. This will reduce market values, and dividends will be low, making it difficult to rerate higher. If vessel values move down and the discount is maintained or even increases, the stock could trade substantially lower than current levels.

The second risk I see is the discretionary quarterly reserve in the dividend calculation. If the company decides to increase the discretionary reserve and not pay substantial dividends, the discount will be difficult to close.

Conclusion

Seanergy Maritime is the only capesize pure play trading at a substantial discount to NAV at a time when rates are the highest they have been in Q1 for many years, and there are good prospects for the year.

With the company’s exposure to the capesize index, it is perfectly positioned to benefit from such rates. If those rates translate to higher dividends, I expect the stock to rerate higher.

data.title: The AI Peak Is An Illusion As Compute Bottleneck Worsens
data.description:
Semiconductor Stocks Have Blasted Through The Roof

Semiconductor investors are testing the market’s patience right now.

I’ve decided to show you the print first, as I believe the proof is in the pudding.

You have to see it to believe it. Even a cursory glance over the VanEck Semiconductor ETF's (SMH) chart, aligning your observation to the price action on the rightmost print.

Truth be told, I’ve not seen such a powerful rally in just four weeks for a while now.

Not only has SMH broken decisively above the 2025 resistance zones (bounded by the $430 level), but it has also easily surpassed the extent of the upside seen in the recent recoveries of the past two years.

I annotated those past recoveries in yellow boxes for your convenience.

I think you would agree with me that they looked like a "relatively slow grind" way back up (as compared to April 2026's vertical print), even though they aren't, in fact.

Whether it’s an opportune moment for us to get on board now (if you missed the April lows) is a question that I will entertain later.

AI Agents Are Now Front And Center

But first up, I guess we must first ask whether the market has simply gone bonkers or whether this is a consequence of the “true” start of the AI agentic era?

That matters? Of course it does. We have been talking about AI agents since 2025, yeah?

Anyone who paid attention to this April 2025 podcast featuring Meta (META) CEO Mark Zuckerberg will know that he has been talking about AI agents since early 2025.

Key to his prediction is that AI agents could be ready for prime time “in the next 12 to 18 months.”

So, have we arrived? Need I say more, really?

Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang (NVDA) has already given us the clues, no?

Anthropic's Growth Inflection Now Key To Semis Fortunes

In a podcast earlier this month, Jensen Huang was absolutely clear in his characterization of Google’s renaissance (GOOGL, GOOG) with the TPU.

All thanks to Anthropic (ANTHRO).

The bigger question is whether this “unique instance” could be parlayed into something bigger benefiting the whole semiconductor ecosystem and not just Nvidia.

Based on how quickly SMH has surpassed its previous 2025 highs, I guess the answer from the market is crystal clear. No doubt.

Or how about Amazon (AMZN)? We know the one party (Anthropic) that has finally come out of the shadows inking all these AI compute deals with these hyperscalers right now, as Dario Amodei and his team are desperate for capacity.

They simply don’t have enough capacity, as the recent “tactics” used clearly show us why.

Things like "lowering rate limits during peak hours, etc." are being tried to see how they could manage that AI compute bottleneck right now.

Which is why I think any of us thinking that AI compute is doomed for 2026 are really not facing the reality.

In fact, AI compute is getting even more profitable right now, as Nvidia chips are being priced more expensively.

We are talking about the H100, yeah? I’m not referring to GB200 or GB300, just in case you were wondering.

What does this suggest to us? It’s really all hands on deck.

Moving From Just Nvidia As The Main Beneficiary

Think about it. You only need to refer to the table of the most valuable semiconductor companies (as of April 27) by market cap that are listed in the U.S.

For the next couple of years, we are looking at adjusted EPS growth that’s no less than 20% CAGR for any of them.

Obviously, Micron (MU) tops the whole list.

But I don’t think that kind of growth is ultimately sustainable.

Which is why I think it has never gotten enough conviction to elevate its so-called “cheap valuations” of <6x on forward earnings to levels that are more consistent with the semiconductor industry average.

Nevertheless, it has also outperformed the rest of its peers hands down here (with 3Y CAGR >100%), so congratulations to MU investors.

In that case, while I could be right (eventually), I was definitely too early in assessing the normalization phase. And there are no marks for being early in the investing game, as the upside was completely missed.

That's fine; I'm not here to win a prize.

I'm here to outperform the market, and my subscribers in Ultimate Growth Investing know very well we have still managed to do that.

On to Jensen Huang.

For Nvidia investors, while the relative pessimism also saw the stock consolidate for six months from late 2025, it has also gotten back above the $5 trillion mark once again.

Along the way, it also touched new highs. So, it does look like normal service has resumed?

Intel’s renaissance (INTC) has also received plaudits, right?

Now, the market is thinking about the potential need for one CPU for every GPU, which could significantly bolster the TAM through 2030.

I don’t think anyone could definitively nail down that these prognostications are 100% accurate, but the market is already allowing it to play out, right?

Well, INTC is up more than 100% YTD.

Companies like Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) will also have significant opportunities, and it won't just be Intel if the formerly much-maligned CPU is now back in the game.

I guess the complete retooling of all CPUs into GPUs was, after all, not fully baked in reality. Or one could argue that market developments shift rapidly as well.

AI agentic inferencing is the clear motivator, nothing less.

What Peak? The World Needs More AI Compute Right Now

In any case, the whole semiconductor value chain is now primed to get bolstered by this AI agentic inference opportunity that we can surely validate by the growth inflection of Anthropic’s ARR run rate.

And because Anthropic serves the enterprise customers (the more “serious” developers) more than the casual consumers, it makes sense that they see tremendous value in Claude Code.

It also makes sense that consumption-based pricing is now becoming the norm, as compute capacity becomes constrained again.

Plus, these tokens are turning increasingly more valuable, as AI companies or the hyperscalers can start pricing for usage and not simply per seat.

Meaning, the revenue potential has now gotten a lot more appealing, right?

Monetization economics have certainly improved.

It’s not that tokens are suddenly a lot cheaper, but now they can be priced more effectively.

And what does that mean?

If you ask me honestly, I’m pretty sure that the market isn’t even close to reaching the saturation point in AI capital outlays, and 2027 wouldn’t be the absolute peak.

Will it be a hockey stick inflection? Not likely, and I think that’s what the market is pricing in now, right?

What’s the semiconductor industry's P/E printing now? Just over 22x. Like, seriously?

There’s a lack of conviction that this rush of AI buildout could carry on sustainably.

And that might be precisely what the market needs to keep this rally going, as the AI “wall of worry” persists.

Nevertheless, I didn’t say that semi stocks aren’t overdue for a pullback.

After such a magnificent rally shooting straight up into overbought levels, a retracement is more than welcome. In fact, I would argue, necessary.

Thus, I will definitely be waiting for that glorious opportunity to add exposure and am patient enough to wait for the print to come back to me.

data.title: Keyence: Positive On Growth Acceleration And Capital Policy Clarity
data.description:
I'm still a bull on Keyence (KYCCF).

KYCCF delivered faster earnings growth in 4QFY25. The company refers to fiscal 2025 as the 12-month period ended March 2026.

It has also provided more details about cash deployment plans recently. This should ease any concerns about an under-utilized balance sheet.

My earlier January 30 article highlighted its potential for better shareholder returns and the above-consensus Q3FY25 showing.

4Q Surprise Led To Stock Price Surge

The group published an announcement revealing its most recent performance on Friday, April 24.

KYCCF's EPS was 25% higher YoY for Jan-Mar '26. That's a big improvement from 3QFY2025's +9% and 4QFY2024's +4%. According to S&P Capital IQ, its latest three-month bottom-line of JPY553/share was 17% ahead of the sell-side's projections.

My take is that the firm's Q4 beat benefited from further penetration into non-Japanese markets, a broader product line-up, and leaner operations.

Its 4QFY25 domestic sales rose 8.6% YoY, which was on par with the 8.5% increase in the same timeframe a year ago.

KYCCF's international turnover expansion accelerated from 4QFY24's +9.6% and 3QFY25's +17.2% to +23.3% last quarter. I've identified the European division as a standout, achieving a YoY revenue increase of +25.8%.

The company's results release noted its observations of "recovery signs in Europe following a prolonged period of cautious capital spending." This region's verticals that did well included "electronics, pharmaceuticals, and machine tools."

KYCCF also drew attention to the newly launched "3D printers and smart valves" in its Q4 disclosure. The former commands a price premium over its other solutions. The latter is bundled with core sensor offerings to enhance customer stickiness.

It's easy for me to see why these portfolio additions have boosted both its growth and profitability.

Separately, KYCCF's EBIT-to-sales widened by 380bps from 49.6% for 3QFY2025 to 53.6% in 4QFY2025. During the same period, its SG&A-to-revenue declined 3.4ppts to 29.9%.

The enterprise will only disclose its end-FY25 staff strength in its annual report later. Given the lower SG&A ratio, I expect the headcount increase last year to be substantially less than the mid-to-high teens CAGR seen in FY21-23. It's likely to have moved past the most aggressive hiring phase.

I think investors were impressed with KYCCF's outperformance. Its OTC and Japan-listed shares jumped 11.3% and 15.8%, respectively, on Monday post-earnings.

Sending A Shareholder-Friendly Signal By Putting Surplus Capital To Work

My late-Jan '26 piece predicted that a "dividend payout ratio (raise) is very likely, considering the Japanese regulatory authorities' scrutiny of cash utilization." I was proven to be right.

KYCCF's FY2025 cash distribution was lifted by 57% to JPY550/share. This implies that the dividend-to-earnings ratio rose from FY24's 21.3% to the recent fiscal year's 30.0%.

Management committed to "stable and continuous dividend payments" in its earnings announcement. This sends me a clear message that its payout should either be maintained or increased going forward.

I wrote in a prior September 19, 2025, update that "Keyence is hoarding excessive cash." Its current cash-to-market cap is still an elevated 17% (source: S&P Capital IQ). My opinion is that its capital allocation approach has changed for the better, starting with this dividend hike.

SA News reported on April 27 that KYCCF "will seek investor approval to amend its charter to enable buybacks." I believe this is the right time to contemplate the purchase of its own shares. The stock is now trading at 20.8x consensus FY2027 EV/EBITDA. Its five-year mean and peak multiples were more demanding at 26.4x and 44.2x, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ.

Organic Investment Plans

As per the chart presented above, KYCCF will build a distribution hub that should come online towards the latter part of next year. I'm encouraged by the management's confidence to opt for a bigger facility. Also, this Takatsuki asset is intended to facilitate the rapid distribution of consumables and spare parts for its new 3D printers. These are higher-ASP items that can widen its group-level margins, as I've mentioned earlier.

The firm also stated in its Q4 slides that "we will proactively consider M&A opportunities." My September 2025 write-up explained how the previous "acquisition of software developer Cadenas" places "KYCCF in a position to develop new value-added and higher-margin solutions." I have a favorable view of its greater willingness to seek out value-accretive inorganic deals.

I infer from KYCCF's recent moves and comments that it's becoming more pro-investor. The increased transparency regarding its cash management suggests that excess capital will be deployed in a value-enhancing manner.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Bear-Case Scenarios

A global recession might prompt the company's customers to cut back on CAPEX. That will hurt the sales of its factory automation-related products.

KYCCF may overpay for inferior acquisition targets or invest in new solutions with limited potential. These actions would most probably be perceived by the market to be value-destructive.

My Concluding Thoughts

I've kept KYCCF as a "Buy"-rated name.

The stock's EV/EBITDA ratio could expand by +27% (26.4/20.8) benchmarked with its historical trading average.

Looking ahead, I see multiple catalysts, such as the commencement of buybacks, synergistic acquisitions, and a further payout rise.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JPST: Investing In The Ultrashort End Of The Yield Curve
data.description:
The JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF (JPST) is a fixed income strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the ultrashort end of the yield curve across a diversified portfolio of debt securities. JPST can be utilized by investors seeking to reduce duration risk, for interest rate exposure, or as a placeholder in their investment portfolio as an alternative to money market funds. Given that the portfolio leans heavily into investment-grade securities, JPST may be ideal for investors taking a more conservative approach to fixed-income investing.

About the JPMorgan Ultra-Short Income ETF

JPST was launched by JPMorgan Asset Management on May 17, 2017, on the NYSE Arca Exchange. The strategy has a low fee of 18bps, aligned with peer ETFs in this market. JPST exhibits substantial liquidity with $37b in net assets, with an average of $349mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, and has a median bid/ask spread of 0.02%, minimizing costs associated with entering and exiting a position.

JPST pays out a monthly distribution with the payout of $2.19/share over the last twelve months, yielding 4.32%. Given the structure of the ETF and low duration risk, the distribution may exhibit greater sensitivity to interest rate policy set by the Federal Reserve, particularly when considering that the average maturity is 1.11 years.

With a portfolio duration of 0.83 years, the price of the bonds held in the ETF will exhibit minimal sensitivity to changes in interest rate policy when compared to longer-duration bonds. This effectively means that though the price of the bonds may not change, the value of the cash flows, in time, may shift in value based on Fed rate policy.

JPST is made up of investment-grade securities, with the majority of the portfolio geared towards A- and BBB-rated issuances, making up 35% and 25% of the portfolio, respectively. While credit quality may be less of a hindrance for short-duration issuances, the investment-grade approach can provide investors with more certainty that the strategy will function as intended during most market environments. Accordingly, the ETF invests across 774 individual issuances, with 64% of the portfolio being made up of investment-grade corporate bonds with additional exposures spread across certificates of deposit, commercial paper, treasuries, and asset-backed securities.

Per the Prospectus, the fund may elect to rotate into high-yield or unrated issuances in order to garnish yield. The fund may also rotate into other securities like mortgage-backed securities (MBSs) and collateralized loan obligations (CLOs).

Investor Suitability

JPST can be utilized by a wide variety of investors seeking exposure to the ultrashort end of the yield curve. JPST can be utilized to reduce duration risk as part of a diversified fixed income portfolio. The strategy may also be utilized as a defensive position with the expectation of a shift in interest rate policy or a challenging equity market environment. JPST can also be utilized as a placeholder for unallocated funds, earning a monthly yield rather than holding cash in a money market fund at a lower rate.

Risks Related to JPST

JPST is a relatively low-risk fixed income strategy given its short-duration exposure and investment-grade allocation. Cuts to the key interest rate by the Federal Reserve may be the biggest risk the ETF may face, not necessarily in terms of duration risk, but cash flow risk when new issuances are cycled in.

Final Thoughts

JPST can be utilized by a wide variety of investors seeking to earn yield on short-duration fixed-income securities. The strategy exhibits relatively low duration and credit risk but has the option of rotating into higher-risk assets if gaining exposure is necessary. JPST can be used to lower an investor’s duration risk as part of a fixed income strategy or simply as a placeholder for unallocated funds, providing for a versatile product.

This article answers these three questions about JPST:

  • Where does JPST fall on the risk spectrum?
  • How can JPST best be utilized by investors?
  • How frequently does JPST make distributions?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: Zijin Mining: Still A Bull After Q1 Beat
data.description:
I'm staying positive on Zijin Mining Group Company Limited (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY) (2899.HK).

The near-doubling of ZIJMF's 1Q26 net profit was a positive surprise. I believe the favorable growth trajectory can be maintained, considering both organic and inorganic drivers.

The focus of my June 13, 2025, initiation piece was ZIJMF's proposed equity carve-out. The 85%-owned subsidiary, Zijin Gold (ZJGIY) (2259.HK), has subsequently been listed in Hong Kong since the end of September '25.

Q1 Earnings Jump Exceeded Expectations

The firm revealed its latest financials this past Tuesday.

ZIJMF's Jan-Mar '26 bottom line came in 98% higher YoY at CNY20.1B. That represented a better-than-anticipated 26% of the prior full-year consensus forecast (source: Bloomberg).

In my opinion, the solid first-quarter showing was driven by rising metal prices, production ramp-up, and greater operational discipline.

The ASPs for ZIJMF's core copper- and gold-related assets rose 35% and 65%, respectively, in 1Q2026 on a YoY basis.

A commodity-focused fund highlighted "copper's importance in infrastructure" and the "tight supply-demand fundamentals" in its late-March investor letter. Separately, SA contributor "Eugenio Cantone" has described gold as "the most reliable safe-haven asset" in turbulent times like these. It's easy for me to understand why the two metals have done so well recently.

The group's 1Q26 mined gold output also expanded by 23% YoY to 24 metric tons. I think that its inorganic moves have paid off. ZIJMF bought two projects in Central Asia and West Africa last year. These additions are starting to drive up volumes in early 2026.

The gold portfolio's sales outperformance helped to offset a 10% YoY drop in copper-related volume for the recent three-month period. But I expect a turnaround in subsequent quarters. The company's Tibetan mine called "Julong" came online in January this year and supported a Q1 QoQ growth of 2% for its copper assets.

I'm impressed with how ZIJMF has been able to keep the expense increases modest too. The per-unit costs for its copper and gold segments were only up 12% and 10% YoY, respectively, for the 1st quarter of '26. That's much less meaningful than the output surge during the same timeframe.

Anticipating Sustained Momentum For The Full Year

The enterprise's FY2025 net income attributable to shareholders went up 62% to CNY51.8B. My bet is on a similar growth in the low-60s percentage range this year, given the encouraging 1Q head start.

I've identified two major financial improvement levers that are expected to materialize in the remainder of 2026.

One of them is a key M&A deal.

"Allied Gold (AAUC) shareholders voted in favor of the proposed C$5.5B buyout by" ZIJMF as per an April 1 SA News article. According to SA analyst "Mountain Valley Value's" analysis, AAUC is an African "multi-asset gold producer" whose "production is set to double over the coming five years."

My projections point to its daughter company ZJGIY's gold volume expansion accelerating from FY25's +20% to FY26's +36%. This assumes that AAUC's takeover is concluded before the end of 1H2026 as planned.

On the other hand, ZIJMF is making good progress in diversifying beyond gold/copper.

It produced 16k metric tons of "Lithium Carbonate Equivalent" or "LCE" in the first three months of the year. That's equivalent to a more than 11-fold increase over 1Q2025.

ZIJMF has multiple projects to support the targeted scale-up to 120k mt of LCE in full-year 2026. These include Africa's "Manono", South America's "Tres Quebradas", and China's "Xiangyuan" mines.

In the preceding section, I touched on the new "Julong" asset. This is another critical growth driver. ZIJMF's goal is to have the property deliver 300-350k mt of copper this year. That will imply a further ramp-up from Q1's 60k mt.

Stronger Balance Sheet Allows For More Generous Dividends

On the day of its results release, the company disclosed its FY26-28 "Profit Distribution and Return Plan."

I like the raising of the payout floor from 30% previously to 35% in the intermediate term.

"Free Cash Flow/FCF" grew 214% YoY to CNY15B in 1Q26. It utilized the surplus capital to lower the net debt-to-equity from 56% as of end-2025 to 42% as of end-March 2026. The healthier credit profile means the firm has more leeway to reward its shareholders.

As per S&P Capital IQ, ZIJMF's consensus FY2026/2027 dividend yields are decent at 3.0% and 3.2%, respectively. It's well-placed to broaden its stockholder base by attracting income-focused investors.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

The group's FY26 earnings might fall short of expectations in the event of a drastic drop in metal prices.

The integration of AAUC's acquired assets might not proceed well due to either management conflicts or regulatory delays.

My Concluding Remarks

I've left my existing 'Buy' rating for this name unchanged.

My target P/E for ZIJMF is 14.6x, pegged to the average multiple for its Chinese peers Tongling (000630.SZ) and CMOC (CMCLF) (603993.SS).

The stock boasts a potential upside of +33% (14.6/11), taking into account its current price-to-earnings of 11.0x (source: S&P Capital IQ).

I see the valuation gap between ZIJMF and its listed comparables narrowing when it achieves significant bottom-line growth again this year.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: BP Q1 Results Show Progress, Not A Buy Signal
data.description:
BP p.l.c.’s (BP) Q1 2026 numbers make it hard to lean too bullish or bearish. There’s clear progress in delivering $3.2 billion of underlying RC profit, $8.9 billion of adjusted operating cash flow and high operating reliability, even though reported operating cash flow was held back by working capital. However, a lot still hangs in the balance: how they manage the big shift in strategy, whether they can actually close planned asset sales, and their constant exposure to swings in oil and gas prices.

My earlier view on BP was Hold, with caution tied to buybacks being suspended and extra cash being directed toward debt reduction. Since then, BP’s shares have actually risen more than 20%, while the stock is up 33.6% YTD; one news headline also described the stock as outperforming rivals since the Iran-war period. That’s come alongside profits bouncing back quarter-over-quarter, strong results in its downstream trading and a solid approach to managing capital. The real question is whether these gains are the start of something lasting or just a brief improvement while BP is still figuring out how to balance its old business with new investments.

Profit Rebound

BP’s Q1 underlying replacement cost profit hit $3.2 billion, jumping up from last quarter. The main reason was exceptional oil trading, stronger midstream performance, and a $1.7 billion quarter-over-quarter increase in products, while oil production and operations was broadly flat quarter-over-quarter and gas and low-carbon energy was slightly lower. Profit came in at $3.8 billion, much better than the loss posted in Q4 and about 5.6x what it was a year ago. Total revenue reached $52.26 billion, coming in $6.3 billion above what analysts expected. Earnings per share (ADS) landed at $1.24, beating estimates by more than 30 cents. These numbers point to BP getting a lot right in a tough, unpredictable energy market, especially by keeping upstream plant reliability at 95.7% and refining availability at 96.3%, with refining availability above BP’s 96% target for the fifth consecutive quarter.

Adjusted operating cash flow before working capital came in strong at $8.9 billion. But if you look at actual operating cash flow, it dropped to $2.9 billion. The main reason: a $6.0 billion build-up in working capital, mostly from stacking up downstream inventories for the season and effects from price swings. Management thinks around half of the $4.1 billion seasonal, inventory and rising-price component should unwind over the rest of the year, with most of that over the next two quarters, though that depends on how things shake out, both with geopolitics and how fast inventory levels get back to normal.

Production Holds

Production landed at 2,339 mboed, holding steady even with all the Middle East supply chain disruptions and after a North Sea divestment. Upstream got a lift from higher prices, BP averaged $59.75 a barrel on oil production and operations liquids and $67.17 a barrel on gas and low-carbon energy liquids. Brent sat at $81.1, but those realized prices still trailed the main benchmarks. That's mostly down to price lags, price caps, timing of liftings and contract structures; management pegs it at “around $700 million from price lags” on oil and $200 million for gas, if you match it up with their usual rules of thumb. Bottom line, if those lags keep up or get worse, future revenue probably won’t match the more optimistic spot price hopes.

Cost Cuts

Costs stayed under control. Underlying operating expenses were $5.369 billion, down from $5.639 billion last quarter, or roughly 5% lower quarter-over-quarter. That’s mostly from trimming overhead, pushing through ongoing cost cuts and sticking to the plan to slim down the portfolio. The Gelsenkirchen refinery sale, which BP announced back in March, will bump up their cost-cutting target to somewhere between $6.5 and $7.5 billion by 2027. That’s a real lever for them as they try to boost returns and make up for the drag from the energy transition. High reliability in upstream and refining, over 95%, helped too. That gave BP more operational leverage since they’re clearly putting cash generation first and holding off on less disciplined spending for now.

Balance Sheet

The BP balance sheet is still the main thing holding back the stock in the short run. Net debt climbed to $25.3 billion from $22.2 billion last quarter, primarily driven by lower operating cash flow after the working-capital build, with major divestment proceeds still expected later in 2026. Management still says it expects $9-$10 billion from selling assets in 2026, with about $6 billion from the Castrol deal supposed to close by the end of 2026 and 2026 divestment proceeds significantly weighted to the second half. That schedule matters: if any of it slips, BP will have a harder time getting net debt down into the $14-$18 billion range it’s aiming for by 2027.

Unless BP suddenly starts throwing off a lot more cash or those asset sales speed up, buybacks probably aren’t coming back anytime soon. That’s what both management and analysts are saying and you see it in the headlines, “BP’s debt boost could dash hopes of return to share buybacks” pretty much sums up the mood right now. Instead, BP’s putting capital toward the dividend.

BP’s 2026 plan keeps capital spending steady, landing between $13 and $13.5 billion. That’s down from last year’s $14.5 billion, with spending expected to be evenly weighted through the year. They’re using a revised $82.80/bbl Brent (CO1:COM) price assumption for 2026 impairment testing, while $81.1/bbl was the Q1 Brent average and expect production to stay about the same, mostly because issues in the Middle East are still weighing things down. Segment breakdown: gas and low-carbon output pull numbers down a bit, but oil production holds steady, refining benefits from a significantly lower level of turnaround activity, while refining margins remain sensitive to the cost of supply and conditions in the Middle East. BP says the underlying effective tax rate and results remain sensitive to the volatility of the price environment and its impact on the geographic mix of profits and losses. Management also counts on working capital coming back on schedule; if it doesn’t, cash flow and leverage numbers get tight.

Valuation

BP shares trade at about 9.12x forward earnings. The market seems to expect cash flow to hold up, assets to sell on time, and dividends to stay covered. Surprises could help, like selling Castrol or Gelsenkirchen sooner than planned, stronger results in trading or refining, or getting back to share buybacks if net debt drops as planned. On the other hand, delays in asset sales, worse Middle East problems (either from more lost volumes or lower prices), or transition write-downs that don’t get offset somewhere else could all hit cash flow and threaten the dividend.

Market Mood

Doubts remain, not helped by recent governance and shareholder-pressure headlines, including the chair’s re-election, rejected board proposals and climate-disclosure opposition. Management, though, is talking a bit differently now. CFO Kate Thomson said,

“As I reflect on another strong quarter, we are building momentum, delivering on our financial plan, all in support of our strategy. We are continuing to high-grade our portfolio and are taking action to reduce our cost base…We remain on track to deliver on our key targets by the end of 2027.”

That message leans on operational follow-through, but it’s still clear that the guidance depends on actually hitting those future milestones.

Risks

When commodity prices swing, realized prices, refining margins, the underlying tax rate, production and cash flow can all move because BP says its outlook remains sensitive to commodity volatility, price lags, contract structures and Middle East conditions. Timing on working capital and selling off assets can mess with cash from operations and make dividends less steady. Legal and regulatory headaches haven’t gone away, there are lawsuits in Kenya and over projects in the U.S. Gulf, for example.

Operations aren’t bulletproof, either. Supply chains are still shaky, especially with what’s happening in the Middle East. If it takes longer than planned to sell assets, net debt could remain harder to bring down toward the $14-$18 billion end-2027 target, which would weigh on the path back to larger cash returns.

Looking ahead to the second half of 2026 and into 2027, there are three main things to watch: whether the company actually closes on the asset sales it’s already announced (Castrol matters most here), whether free cash flow can consistently cover the dividend and then either a real pickup in share buybacks or a clear move to bring net debt down. If working capital loosens up like management says it should and asset sales go through like planned, BP might deserve a higher valuation. But if they start slipping on cash management, can’t pay down debt, or have to write down assets again, there’s still room for the stock to drop, especially if interest rates or commodity prices don’t help.

Button line, a Hold rating still fits here, not because I don’t trust BP’s ability to generate cash, but because the shift from fixing the balance sheet and selling assets to actually delivering higher, steady returns just isn’t finished yet. Any progress, whether from cost cuts, selling stuff off, or running the business better, has to show up clearly in earnings, cash flow and net debt trends. I’d upgrade BP to Buy if I saw real, steady growth in free cash flow and debt coming down, or if they got serious about share buybacks again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Universal Electronics Has Multiple Paths To A Rebound
data.description:
A little over a year and a half ago, I made the bull case for Universal Electronics (UEIC), historically a provider of TV remote controls, but seeking to make a pivot towards the connected home electronics space.

With the stock trading at a discount to book, with a reasonable turnaround plan and a litigation catalyst to boot, shares appeared at the time to be a great opportunity.

Yet while UEIC appeared to be embarking on a comeback in the months following my write-up, since 2025 shares have coughed back these gains, and then some.

Instead of bouncing back from $8.50 per share to well into the double digits, the stock has since fallen by over 50%. Yet while this underfollowed and under-loved stock still gives off plenty of "value trap vibes," there may be merit in entering a position at current prices.

For one, there has been progress on both the patent litigation front, as well as with UEIC's transformation efforts. Valuation-wise, the stock remains considerably cheap, possibly making it an ideal buyout candidate for a strategic buyer, if and/or when the company makes further progress with its connected home transformation.

Finally, even if the transformation remains years away from fully taking shape, incremental progress, in the form of cost reductions leading to improved profitability, could also give this stock a meaningful life within a reasonable time frame.

With this, let's dive in and explore why it may prove worthwhile to take a second chance on Universal Electronics.

Recap and Recent Developments

In a nutshell, he's my initial bull case for UEIC stock: after years of steady revenue and earnings decline, due to the "cord cutting" trend, Universal Electronics knew full well that it needed to get out of the TV remote business.

Hence, the company began its pivot into HVAC and home automation technology products. While this did not result in a rebound in overall revenue, it did lead to an increase in gross margins, as well as a reduction in operating losses.

Again, alongside the turnaround catalyst, there was also a litigation-based catalyst as well. A favorable ruling in a lawsuit paved the way for Universal Electronics to pursue money damages against Roku (ROKU) and its TV manufacturing partners for past infringement on UEIC's patents.

Atop these two catalysts, there was also a factor that could be considered both a potential catalyst as well as a potential risk for the stock: the growing involvement of activist investor Eric Singer in UEIC, through Toro 18 Holdings, an entity owned by Singer's Immersion (IMMR). Immersion is a haptic technology patent licensing company that also makes investments in other public and privately held companies.

Given Immersion's track record of acquiring control of struggling businesses like Barnes & Noble Education (BNED) through restructuring, its growing involvement may not necessarily be to the benefit of existing UEIC shareholders.

Flash forward to now, and we'll take a look at each of these factors and how each played a role in UEIC's lackluster price performance since my 2024 writeup.

The Turnaround

Taking a look at Universal's 2024 full-year financials, it's easy to see why UEIC shares traded upward in the months following my initial article.

Although sales declined by around 6% that year, from $420.5 million to $394.9 million, operating losses shrank from $24.1 million to $7.2 million. EBITDA swung from negative $1.7 million in 2023 to deep in the black, $10.1 million, in 2024. Gross margins improved YoY, from 25.1% to nearly 29%.

Unfortunately, in 2025, progress was a bit more mixed. Yes, connected home sales reached $125.4 million, up 15.7%. For the year, the company also reported positive operating income, as well as positive non-GAAP earnings of $4.2 million, or 31 cents per share.

However, with sales for the legacy entertainment business falling more than expected, overall sales took a 6.7% dive for the year. As management puts it, the commenced home business still has yet to reach an inflection point.

Alongside a stalling out in terms of fiscal performance, another factor casting doubt on the UEIC turnaround has been about the company's current leadership, or lack thereof.

A year ago, longtime CEO Paul Arling retired. COO Richard Carnifax became interim CEO in June 2025, a role he continues to fill, as Universal has yet to appoint a permanent CEO.

Activist Involvement

Upon Arling's exit, Eric Singer became UEIC's board Chairman, further indicating Immersion's de facto control of the company. So far, Immersion has yet to make any public announcements indicating an interest in acquiring this company outright.

Interestingly enough, the two companies haven't even formed any partnerships, collaborations, or any sort of arrangement to monetize the patent portfolio. Singer, at last publicly, hasn't even made an effort to try and sell the company to a potential buyer.

Roku Litigation

UEIC's turnaround may be happening slower than expected, but at least the litigation catalyst has continued to play out favorably. As discussed in the company's latest 10-K filing, the court and the PTAB continued to rule in Universal Electronics' favor, with the PTAB invalidating the last of Roku's infringement claims. With these out of the way, and with the patent court already ruling in UEIC's favor years back, the potential for this company to receive damages remains on the table.

However, if a settlement is not reached, the case is scheduled to proceed to trial next March, as interim CEO Carnifax mentioned on the Q3 2025 earnings call. It's unclear how much Universal could receive in a settlement or judgment, but given its relatively small market cap, even an eight-figure amount could move the needle.

Valuation

Currently, Universal Electronics has a market cap of $53 million. The company has a total debt of $33.5 million, most of which is covered by the company’s $32.3 million cash position, giving UEIC an enterprise value of around $54.2 million.

Given the company’s TTM EBITDA of $9.7 million, the stock trades at a super-low EV/EBITDA of 5.6x. This represents a sharp valuation discount not just to other consumer electronics stocks, but also to profitable connected home electronics companies like Resideo Technologies (REZI), which trades at an EV/EBITDA ratio of around 11x.

Not only that, I could see a strategic buyer being willing to buy the company at full value, or around twice the current share price, and still find a way to make it accretive, given the considerable overlapping costs that could be eliminated by consolidation.

Having said this, I don't believe that UEIC, under Immersion's control, will be looking at selling the company anytime soon. At least, not until the aforementioned Roku litigation is resolved. Even then, UEIC may be interested in pursuing further patent monetization activities. Singer/Immersion's cost basis is also well above current trading prices. I don't see Singer making an exit just to break even.

Moreover, I don't envision a lot of buyers wanting to gobble up Universal Electronics in its current "as is" condition. Much of the company's sales and earnings continue to come from the rapidly declining home entertainment business. It may not be until Universal is more of a connected home pure play that the company gets acquired by a larger U.S. or Asian-based competitor.

Potential Catalysts

Litigation Leads to Cash Windfall/Special Dividend

If UEIC's litigation against Roku results in a legal judgment or settlement, perhaps in the tens of millions or even hundreds of millions, the resultant cash windfall could change the story here for shares.

An amount at the lower end of the range could end up being used to pay a special dividend/buy back shares. A higher amount could be used either to pay down debt or to bulk up Universal's connected home electronics business.

Latest Product Launch Means Better-than-Expected Results

At the most recent Consumer Electronics Show (CES 2026) last January, UEIC unveiled its latest Next-Generation QuickSet homesense connected home application. Management is playing it conservative regarding this year's growth for the connected home business, but developments like this could mean better-than-expected growth for the segment in the coming quarters.

Better-Than-Expected Results, Even if "Inflection Point" Not Reached

Beyond the potential for new products to increase the chances of an inflection point happening, or at the very least drive better-than-expected growth, there's also the prospect of Universal delivering better-than-expected earnings, even as the legacy business keeps declining, and growth for the connected home business takes a breather.

Universal Electronics continues to right-size its two businesses and is engaging in further cost-reduction activities. Although management is not providing quarterly guidance for 2026, CFO Wade Jenke did mention on the latest earnings conference call that non-GAAP earnings are expected to come in between 45 cents and 65 cents per share, or 45% to around 110% above 2025's non-GAAP earnings of 31 cents per share.

If this pans out, this could dramatically improve the stock price in one of two ways. First, if earnings hit this range, the stock could receive a re-rating just based on its new forward P/E.

Instead of trading for 11.8 times forward earnings of 35 cents per share, the stock could trade at a mid-teens multiple on 45 cents to 65 cents, assuming modest valuation expansion. In other words, even without a takeover bid or positive patent suit-related news, there may be a path for UEIC to re-hit prices ranging from $5 to $10 per share.

Risks to Consider

Besides the risk that cost-cutting doesn't solve issues with home entertainment and new product launches fail to maintain the connected home's growth momentum, again there is the risk that Immersion, UEIC's influential shareholder, gains further ownership/control of the company without buying out outside investors at a premium.

Granted, it may be unfair to judge Immersion and its principals just on the BNED deal, but, as mentioned previously, it does serve as a sort of red flag for this stock.

The Bottom Line on UEIC Stock

While the situation with Universal Electronics has played out more as a value trap than as a turnaround play, if you've yet to enter a position, now may be an opportune time.

There's now less uncertainty surrounding the litigation catalyst. Even modest progress with the turnaround/transformation could also drive a big jump for shares.

Thinking longer term, the ties between Immersion and UEIC, previously viewed as possibly bad news for investors, could actually prove beneficial. With Singer at the helm of both companies, the investor, if opting not to fold UEIC into Immersion, could still utilize his talents/experience in the area of patent investing to better monetize UEIC's portfolio.

Put it all together, and the takeaway is clear. Existing investors may want to hold on, and newfound investors may want to jump in.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Travelers: Not Exciting, Just Reliable
data.description:
The Travelers Companies, Inc. (TRV) is rarely the most exciting insurance stock—but it is often one of the most reliable. TRV is one of the insurance stocks I have covered for years and one of my preferred dividend-paying insurance stocks, although I am not a shareholder—I can’t invest everywhere, unfortunately.

To wrap up briefly from my prior analyses:

  • With two decades of dividend growth, Travelers is not far away from dividend aristocrat status.

  • The company also repurchases its shares actively, with a reduction of more than 66% since 2006.

  • The company sold its Canadian operations in 2025 and redeployed the cash received to repurchase shares during the first quarter of 2026.

It is during that same first quarter that the company announced a 14% dividend increase, marking the 22nd year of consecutive dividend growth.

With all this information, I believe it's time to revisit Travelers' profile. Spoiler alert (I know I always spoil): Travelers, Inc. remains a strong candidate for investors looking for a financial stock.

Back to Normal. And It Shows.

Let's start with the underwriting performance—I am an actuary, so I am biased, even obsessed with the underwriting returns of property and casualty (P&C) insurance companies.

To be honest, Travelers' underwriting performance was exceptional during the first quarter.

The Q1 2026 combined ratio amounted to 88.6%, vs. 102.5% last year, which was affected by California wildfires, as was the case for most of the largest U.S. P&C insurers. Segment-wise, all portfolios performed well:

Q1 2026 Q1 2025
Specialty Lines 83.3% 82.5%
Personal Lines 82.9% 115.2%
Commercial Lines 93.8% 96.2%

The major improvement was in personal lines, with lower catastrophe losses than in Q1 2025 (9.1 pts vs. 40.9 pts impact), mostly from the homeowners' insurance portfolio. The personal lines' underlying combined ratio (i.e., excluding prior-year effects and catastrophe losses) improved by 1.6 points to 78.3%.

Commercial lines also benefited from lower catastrophe losses, while specialty lines remained steadily and boringly profitable, with a combined ratio below 85%. This segment remains the cash cow of the company, even though management has not neglected the other two segments and has succeeded in remediating both personal and commercial lines.

To be honest, the biggest news was the return to normal catastrophe loss levels, which positively impacted Q1 2026 performance.

The second driver of higher earnings was investment growth. Investment income reached $1.0 billion, up from $930 million in Q1 2025. For the quarter, investment earnings represented almost half of pre-tax income ($2.1 billion).

Capital Allocation: No Surprises, Just Execution

When you invest in Travelers, you know where you are going. You invest in a disciplined insurance company that redistributes excess capital over time. 2026 started differently from prior years. Previous years were good in terms of capital allocation; 2026 started excellently.

The quarterly dividend per share was raised by 13.5% from $1.05 to $1.10. But Travelers did not stop there. The company repurchased $1.8 billion of shares, including $700 million from the sale of the Canadian operations, as promised. Travelers does not oversell promises; it simply delivers what it announces. In total, the company returned $2 billion to shareholders during the first quarter.

Valuation: Not Cheap, Not Crazy

Travelers, Inc. is not cheap, and there are reasons for that. First, underwriting performance is more than decent. Even though Travelers' combined ratio does not match Chubb's (CB), Travelers' combined ratio is below 100% over the cycle, i.e., the insurance portfolio is profitable every year. Thanks to this profitability, fueled by highly profitable specialty lines and decent performance in commercial and personal lines, the company has grown its investment portfolio to $103 billion, generating $3.3 billion after tax in 2025, with a conservative allocation.

But what impresses shareholders most is the cash they receive, either indirectly through share repurchases or directly through dividend increases. And 2026 is a very strong year in terms of dividend raises. While I expected a 5% to 7% dividend increase, the company raised the dividend by 13.5%, more than double my expectations. It could be seen as aggressive; I don't think so.

If Travelers increased its dividend at a double-digit level, it is not because management suddenly became aggressive; it is because they believe the dividend remains sustainable, even after the sale of the Canadian operations, which were unprofitable—except for the surety business, which was retained.

Thanks to all of this, investors are willing to pay 13.56x earnings and 1.85x book value. Currently, Travelers is priced at around 9x earnings and 2x book value.

However, Q1 2026 was affected by the sale of the Canadian subsidiary; hence, first-quarter performance was somewhat boosted by this one-off effect.

Even when adjusting for the Canadian operation sale, Travelers does not look unrealistically valued.

To make the valuation discussion more explicit, I frame a simple fair value range based on normalized earnings. In 2024 and 2025, the company delivered post-tax income of $5 billion and $6.3 billion, respectively.

Based on a normalized earnings range of $5 billion to $6 billion, and applying a valuation range of 13x to 14x earnings, the implied market capitalization ranges from $65 billion to $84 billion.

At the current valuation (~$65 billion), the stock sits at the lower end of this range, suggesting limited downside and a potential upside of roughly 20% to 30%, depending on underwriting performance and capital returns.

That’s usually enough for this type of company.

Risks: Boring Does Not Mean Risk-Free

Travelers’ profile looks stable, but several risks remain.

First, catastrophe volatility. Q1 2026 benefited from normalized losses, but a heavy catastrophe year could quickly push the combined ratio above 100%, as seen in prior periods. The company is well equipped to handle catastrophe losses, but the market tends to have a short memory.

Second, personal lines execution. While recent improvements are encouraging, this segment has historically been volatile and remains sensitive to inflation, catastrophe events, and pricing discipline.

Third, investment income sensitivity. A large portion of earnings comes from the investment portfolio; in Q1 2026, it represented almost half of the pre-tax earnings. Lower rates or market volatility could pressure returns.

Finally, valuation risk. At around 9x earnings and about 1.8x book, Travelers is not cheap relative to its own history—on earnings multiples, it may look inexpensive, but on a book value basis, it does not. Any disappointment in underwriting could lead to multiple compression. Investors and the market could quickly react if the company fails to meet expectations.

What Travelers Is - And What It Is Not

Travelers is not Chubb in terms of underwriting performance. The latest Q1 2026 results confirm that Chubb outperformed Travelers. There is no doubt about it—Chubb delivers boringly steady underwriting results.

Travelers is not Progressive (PGR) in terms of premium growth.

Travelers is not Cincinnati (CINF) in terms of investment returns.

Travelers is not Aflac (AFL) in terms of dividend growth.

Travelers is an animal of its own, combining mid-single-digit premium growth, an over-cycle combined ratio around 95%–97%, and a conservative investment allocation. All these strengths have helped create value for shareholders.

For investors looking for balance, Travelers does the job.
